title: About
text:
Hi! I am Cassie Nesheim, a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln majoring in Data Science with a minor in Sociology. I am passionate about using data to uncover insights, tell meaningful stories, and support better decision-making, especially in the world of sports.
I have experience working with Python and R to clean, analyze, and visualize data, along with a strong foundation in statistics. My analysis work goes beyond the basics, incorporating methods like hypothesis testing and other statistical techniques to draw deeper, more reliable, confident conclusions.
As a former gymnast, I bring a unique perspective to sports analytics, particularly in gymnastics. This blog features quite a bit of gymnastics analysis, along with a variety of other sports. View my posts to see some of my work!

title: SEC Women’s Gymnastics Home Judging Bias is Overstated
text:
Gymnastics fans have long speculated about “home bias” in judging. According to the gymternet (the mass of gymnastics enthusiasts on social media), judges are noticeably more lax with their judging and often award “gifts,” or scores that are higher than deserved for the home team. For instance, a step on the landing in a routine is almost always an automatic 0.1 deduction. A routine with only one step and no other errors should score around a 9.85 or a 9.9, but at home that routine might score a 9.95, which is an impossible score.
The question then becomes: do gymnasts actually score better when competing at home, and if so, which teams benefit most?
To understand whether bias could exist, it helps to start in the most dominant conference in college gymnastics, the SEC. Because of its position in the sport, this conference draws intense scrutiny, with fans regularly criticize the conference for rampant home judging bias.
A look at the rankings confirms the SEC’s dominance. Rankings start at week 2 for college gymnastics, as this is first week of the season every team has competed. Since week 2, at least seven of the nine SEC teams have been in the top 10 in all of women’s college gymnastics, demonstrating their powerful command of the sport. If home judging bias exists, it would certainly show up in the SEC.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(ggbeeswarm)
library(ggbump)
library(ggalt)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)

sec_reg <- read_csv("data/sec_reg_season.csv")
rankings <- read_csv("data/rtn_rankings.csv")

sec_away <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "A") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals") |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))
  
sec_home <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "H") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals") |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))

sec <- c("Georgia", "LSU", "Florida", "Kentucky", "Missouri", "Alabama", "Arkansas", "Auburn", "Oklahoma")

rankings <- rankings |>
  filter(Week >= 2) |>
  mutate(Rank = as.numeric(Rank)) |>
  filter(Rank < 11)

sec_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec
  )

sec10_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec,
    Rank <= 10
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=rankings |> group_by(Team) |> filter(n() > 1) |> ungroup(), aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team, color="Non-SEC")) + 
  geom_bump(data=sec_ranks |> group_by(Team) |> filter(n() > 1) |> ungroup(), aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team, color="SEC")) +
  geom_point(data=rankings, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, color="Non-SEC"), size = 2) + 
  geom_point(data=sec_ranks, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, color="SEC"), size = 2) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == min(Week)), aes(x = Week - .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == max(Week)), aes(x = Week + .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Conference", values=c("SEC"="blue", "Non-SEC"="lightgrey"), breaks=c("SEC", "Non-SEC")) +
  scale_y_reverse(limits=c(1, 10), breaks=c(1:10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1, 13.5), breaks=c(2:12)) +
  labs(title="SEC Teams Control Women's College Gymnastics.", 
       subtitle="Since week 2, nearly every top 10 team has been from the SEC, leaving litle room for outsiders.", 
       y= "Rank", 
       x = "Week",
    caption="Source: RoadToNationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )









Most scores in college gymnastics fall between 9.7 and 10.0, a very small range, making differences hard to detect at first glance. Though, each event does have a higher average at home than it does away, especially floor.


Code
away_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_away, aes(x = Event, y = Score)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "seashell2") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 3
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(48.5, 50.05), breaks=seq(48.5, 50.0, by=0.25)) +
  labs(subtitle="Away") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face="bold")
  )

home_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_home, aes(x = Event, y = Score, group = Event)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "seashell2") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  )  +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 3
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(48.5, 50.05), breaks=seq(48.5, 50, by=0.25)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=2.85, xmax=4.6, ymin=49.87, ymax=50.05, fill="white", color="grey", alpha=0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.75, y = 49.97, label="Red line denotes average \nteam score per event", size=2.5, color="red") +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 3.5, xmax = 4.5,
        ymin = 49.43, ymax = 49.62) +
  labs(subtitle="Home") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face="plain")
  )

#to test the significance of floor
t_sec_floor <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "FLOOR")

floor_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_floor)

t_sec_bars <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "BARS")

bars_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_bars)

t_sec_beam <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "BEAM")

beam_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_beam)

t_sec_vault <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "VAULT")

vault_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_vault)

score_distribution <- away_dist + home_dist +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Home Judging Bias Emerges Most Clearly on Floor",
    subtitle = "While the averages show scores for teams to be mostly similar when scored at home vs away, \n scores for the last event are noticeably higher when teams are at home.",
    caption = "Source: Virtius and RoadToNationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

score_distribution









Home floor scores are an entire 1.5 points above away floor scores. In gymnastics, this difference is huge. A standard statistical test confirmed that the floor scoring gap between home and away meets is not random chance, it is a real, meaningful difference. No other event showed significance, meaning that their differences between home and away meets could be due to random chance.
Only floor being significant does make some sense when put into context. When teams are at home, they typically compete floor last, and scores tend to loosen as competitions progress, conceivably benefiting the home team in the final event. So, potentially, higher scores are awarded for routines worse than those at the beginning of competition.
Also, if home bias is a real thing, judges may be trying to overcompensate in the last event to ensure the home team wins. While this does not prove intentional bias, it is a plausible reason that only floor has a noticeable difference when scored away vs at home.
As mentioned, this significant difference in home vs away scoring on floor could be for a multitude of reasons, but if it is just simply that they are more comfortable at home, this home scoring bump would show up significantly in the other events.
These floor differences suggest something may be happening late in meets, but the bigger picture lies in total team scores. Floor might be where it shows up, but that is not the whole story. If home judging bias exists, it should impact entire meets, not just one rotation.
To answer that, team scoring averages at home and away were compared across the SEC. The gap between those two numbers reveals which programs gain the most from competing at home.


Code
sec_ha <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals") |>
  group_by(Team, Location) |>
  summarise(
    team_avg = mean(AA)
  )

sec_ha_dumb <- sec_ha |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Location, values_from = team_avg) |>
  mutate(diff = (H-A))

t_sec_team <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01)

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), yend=Team, x=A, xend=H), color="grey", linewidth = 1.5
  )  + 
  geom_point(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=A, color="A"), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=H, color="H"), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_text(data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=(H+A)/2, label=round(diff,3)), vjust=-0.8, size=3) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=197.74, xmax=198.25, ymin=1, ymax=2, fill="lightgrey", color="grey", alpha=0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 198, y = 1.5, label="Values represent home \nminus away averages", size=3, color="black") +
  scale_color_manual(name="Location", values=c("A"="blue", "H"="purple"), labels=c("A"="Away", "H"="Home")) +
  labs(title="All SEC Teams Benefit from Home Judging Advantage.", 
       subtitle="Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida see a stronger boost, though all other teams score higher at home.", 
       y= "Team", 
       x = "Scores",
    caption="Source: Virtius and RoadToNationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









Top SEC teams receive noticeably higher scores at home than when away, whereas teams in the middle or bottom of the conference (with the exception of Auburn) see minimal differences.
Fans often criticize Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida for egregious home scoring, claiming those teams are being wildly over scored. It is important to keep in mind, these are also the top three teams in all of NCAA women’s gymnastics, so they have the most eyes on them. Also, those at the top often receive the most hate, like people rooting against Tom Brady in the Super Bowl year after year. Fans like the underdog and get frustrated when the top teams continue to perform well.
Though the data for Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida does demonstrate differences in average, when tested for significance using a standard statistical cutoff, where any number below -2 is considered significant, it is revealed that only Oklahoma receives a significant bump in scores at home. Meaning that whether Sooners are at home or away has an impact on how well they will score. Rather, Oklahoma experiences home judging bias.
For all other SEC teams, the differences in home and away scores exist but they are not significant and we cannot say that home judging is affecting how well those teams do.


Code
teams <- c("Florida", "LSU", "Oklahoma", "Auburn", "Georgia", "Arkansas", "Missouri", "Kentucky", "Alabama")
team_t_statistic <- numeric(length(teams))

for (i in 1:length(teams)) {
  team_data <- t_sec_team |>
    filter(Team == teams[i])
  
  team_t_statistic[i] <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=team_data)$statistic
}

team_t_tests <- data.frame(teams, team_t_statistic) |>
  arrange(team_t_statistic)

team_t_tests |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    teams = "Team",
    team_t_statistic = "Statistical Cutoff"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Only Oklahoma's Home Scores Demonstrate Bias",
    subtitle = "While most SEC teams see insignificant differences, only the Sooners gain a measureable advantage at home."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: Virtius and RoadtoNationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "indianred1"),
      cell_text(color = "black")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = team_t_statistic >= 2 | team_t_statistic <= -2)
  )








Only Oklahoma's Home Scores Demonstrate Bias


While most SEC teams see insignificant differences, only the Sooners gain a measureable advantage at home.


Team
Statistical Cutoff




Oklahoma
-2.847534437


Auburn
-1.791664031


LSU
-1.209713471


Florida
-0.911681089


Arkansas
-0.841117949


Georgia
-0.651607535


Missouri
-0.336123599


Alabama
-0.128411112


Kentucky
-0.007233853



Source: Virtius and RoadtoNationals | By: Cassie Nesheim








Home scores across the SEC are, on average, higher than those on the road, especially on floor. But when those differences are tested more closely and each team is examined, the story becomes less dramatic. Only Oklahoma shows a statistically meaningful home advantage, while the rest of the conference does not.
In a conference where dominance is the standard, home judging bias should be easy to detect. Instead, it largely fails to appear.
While fans may continue to debate home bias and argue the home team is being “gifted” scores, aside from the Sooners, their claims have no merit. If home judging bias were widespread, it would likely show up clearly in the top teams, like those in the SEC, but the data suggests otherwise. So, at least for now, gymnastics fans must come to terms with the fact that home scores are slightly higher, with the reassurance that it is not so different that the results of each meet change depending on who is at home.
Home judging bias may exist in isolated cases, but it is far from having the dramatic effect it is often claimed to have on SEC teams.
